Goldwyn, a Polish-American businessman, had met Coco while holidaying in Monte Carlo. Her arrangement with Goldwyn dwindled as the 1930s progressed. Coco's personal life was tumultuous by the time she was visiting Hollywood in the 1930s. DeMille in Hollywood in the 1920s, notably drawing up the designs for the look of Egypt for DeMille's version of the Ten Commandments in 1923. Between 1933 and 1935, Coco helped finance Irib's journal Le Temois, The Witness, a publication which presented the artworks of Irib and others. The 69 issues of the journal, which appeared in the mid-1930s, were highly political, with Irib demonstrating a charged sense of antipathy towards foreigners in France, a pronounced anti-Semitism and an ultra-nationalist platform. As we will see shortly, this appears not to have conflicted with Chanel's own politics by the 1930s. Beyond her sexual relationships, Chanel continued to mix in bohemian and artistic circles in Paris throughout the 1920s and 1930s. These were hedonistic times, and there was one rumor around Paris at the time that Coco had actually acquired her first name in reference to the prolific quantities of cocaine taken at her parties in the 1920s. While this explanation is probably somewhat spurious, we do know that Chanel developed a habit of injecting herself with morphine on a daily basis in the early 1930s. She would remain addicted to opiates for the remainder of her life. The 1930s saw Chanel branching out further again into women's jewelry. In 1932, the company held an exhibition of its first items, one which made clear that this range would certainly be on the higher end of Chanel's products. The show featured products called the Comet and the Fountain, two necklaces containing multiple diamonds. While this show presented material which was not exactly for general purchase, the 1930s were notable for the manner in which Chanel began to incorporate rhinestones and other smaller and more modest gemstones into the fashion house's dresses and products, something which disseminated into other fashion houses in the years that followed. The end result of all of this rampant growth in the 1920s and early 1930s, and the manner in which Chanel had established itself as the preeminent fashion house in not just France, but in Europe, was a company which had a huge revenue, and that employed upwards of 4,000 people in various capacities, as seamstresses, shop employees, and designers. Only one company could rival it by the mid-1930s. This was the house of Schiaparelli, which had been founded in Paris in 1927 by the Italian aristocrat fashion designer Elsa Schiaparelli. Beginning with knitwear and then moving into avant-garde and surrealist design, Schiaparelli soon followed Chanel's lead in associating with some of Paris and France's leading artistic figures in order to promote her products, notably in Schiaparelli's case the artist and filmmaker Salvador Dali and the poet, playwright and visual artist Jean Cocteau. By the early 1930s, Schiaparelli's range had expanded dramatically. Although many individuals lambasted Schiaparelli's designs in the 1930s as being impractical or even bizarre, she was the only designer in France in the 1930s whose influence and reputation matched that of Chanel. Perhaps if history had not unfolded as it did in Europe in the late 1930s to mid-1940s, Coco and the House of Chanel's enormous success might have continued unmarred by controversy. But a chain of events beginning in Germany and the choices she would make in that decade would ultimately cast a shroud over Coco Chanel's legacy. Early in 1933, after a rapid ascent in political popularity following economic turmoil partially caused by the Wall Street crash and the Great Depression, the Nazi party, led by Adolf Hitler, came to power in Germany. They were determined to reverse what they perceived as the humiliation foisted upon Germany at the end of the First World War and to make their nation the foremost power of continental Europe. This would mean a new war, with France being one of the primary targets, not just to recover the provinces of Alsace and Lorraine, which had been lost following the First World War, but also to dominate Germany's western neighbor. Initially, the Nazis had anticipated needing a gradual military build-up to accomplish their aims, intending to wait until 1941 or 1942 to initiate full-scale war. But when the European powers were both sluggish to re-arm and put up little resistance to Germany's annexation of Austria and Czechoslovakia in 1938 and 1939 respectively, Hitler moved the schedule forward and in September 1939 invaded Poland. This was a step too far for both Britain and France, and they declared war on Germany in response. This was the beginning of what would become the Second World War. For Coco Chanel and many other French people, the outbreak of the war in the autumn of 1939 impacted little on their lives to begin with. The fighting was largely confined to Poland, with only limited border skirmishes and forays taking place along the Franco-German border. Hence, while the news of the German occupation of Poland was concerning for many in Paris, beyond the obvious signs of military mobilization and the imposition of rationing, life went on much as it previously had in the final months of 1939 and into early 1940. However, normal life came to a shuddering halt in the late spring, as the Nazis first conducted a blistering campaign against Denmark and Norway, and then proceeded to invade the Low Countries and northeastern France. The speed of the German advance caught both the French military and the British expeditionary force in northern France by surprise, and within weeks the Nazis were closing in on the capital. The French government fled on the 10th of June, and the Germans occupied the city four days later. A collaborationist government negotiated surrender terms, whereby the southern half of France would be ruled by a semi-autonomous government based out of the town of Vichy, where Chanel had spent summers performing in the 1900s. But Paris was to be occupied by the Nazis for the next four years. It was a period during which countless French men and women like Coco Chanel would have to decide whether to resist the regime or collaborate with it. As we will see, Coco Chanel chose the latter. Upon the commencement of war in September 1939, Chanel had released a public statement affirming her belief that a period of renewed warfare across Europe was not a time for high fashion, and that she consequently intended to close all of her shops, except for the flagship enterprise on the Rue de Cambon. This was not necessarily an unreasonable step to take. After all, with rationing being introduced across the continent, and trade networks disintegrating as nations went to war with each other, there would be a lack of raw materials available to make high-end fashion, and few people would have been comfortable investing in conspicuous signs of wealth in such straightened times. But it has also been suggested that Chanel used the outbreak of the war to exact revenge on her employees, many of whom had engaged in disputes over working conditions and pay in 1936, during a general strike amongst the French labor unions. Whatever her motives might have been, we know what fate ultimately befell Chanel's employees. Nearly 4,000 people found themselves out of work from the late autumn of 1939 onwards. The house of Chanel had effectively been furloughed until the end of the war. This has raised questions as to what Chanel was doing, herself, throughout the conflict, especially after the occupation of Paris in the summer of 1940. The answer, revealed by a number of sources in recent times, is not as one would hope for a historical figure of such cultural influence. Chanel would spend much of the war consorting and collaborating with the Nazi regime. Her willingness to countenance the Nazis in France was seen early on when she took up residence in the Hotel Ritz on the Place Vendôme in central Paris. Following the occupation of the French capital in June 1940, the Ritz had quickly become a favorite haunt of the leading members of the occupation Nazi government. Thus, anyone staying here or consorting with other guests was making a clear statement in favor of the German takeover of France and Nazi ideology. Moreover, this interpretation of Chanel's choice in residence is aligned with Chanel's own attested political and racial views by the early 1940s, which were foreshadowed by her relationship with Irib and her support for his ultra-nationalist journal, The Witness, in the 1930s. As such, her decision to shut Chanel's stores and operations across France in the autumn of 1939 has been viewed by some historians as part of an effort to undermine her Jewish business partners. One particular instance of such efforts stands out, her attempt in 1941 to use anti-Semitic laws in Nazi-occupied France to steal company profits from her Jewish business partner, whose descendants, in a twist of fate, would go on to own the Chanel brand to this day. While Chanel was the face and primary owner of the House of Chanel, she had had a significant business partner since the early 1920s. This was Pierre Wertheimer, a scion of a German-Jewish family who had moved from the Alsace region around the Franco-German border to Paris following the outbreak of the Franco-Prussian War in 1870. They remained in the French capital after the swift Prussian victory in that war and Pierre was born in the city in 1888. As a young man in his 20s, Pierre became involved in the cosmetics industry in France, and helped develop a dry rouge for the notable French makeup company Bourgeois. Such was the success of this product that Wertheimer rose quickly within Bourgeois, and in 1917 he and his brother Paul had become directors thereof. It was just a few short years later that Chanel and Wertheimer began negotiations to use Wertheimer's business contacts and industry links to help market Chanel No. 5 globally. Eventually, Chanel, Wertheimer and several other French and Jewish investors in the cosmetics and fragrance industry reached an arrangement in the mid-1920s whereby the Wertheimers would effectively manufacture, distribute and otherwise manage Chanel No. 5. They would receive 70% of the profits in return for effectively running Parfum Chanel. As the subsidiary became known, Coco would become an all-but-silent partner. She would have to do very little henceforth and would receive 10% of all profits based on her intellectual copyright of the fragrance and branding. While many would have been happy with this arrangement, as the Wertheimers expanded the reach of Chanel No. 5 and increased sales in the course of the 1920s and 1930s, Coco became increasingly disgruntled. In 1935, she had already attempted to sue Pierre and his family, but the suit had failed as Coco was trying to dispute the validity of a binding legal agreement, which she had freely entered into years ago. She now set out to use the occupation of France by the Nazis and the pronounced anti-Semitism of the new regime against the Wertheimers and their Jewish heritage. Beginning in 1941, she began petitioning the German occupation government in France to be given sole control of Parfum Chanel, using a Nazi law which allowed people of allegedly Aryan race to take over Jewish businesses. She was also able to claim that the Wertheimers had abandoned the business when they fled to the United States to avoid Nazi persecution in 1940. However, she was surprised to learn in the course of the summer and autumn of 1941 that Wertheimer had taken steps to prevent her seizing the company. Before he and his family fled France, he had transferred his 70% stake, and with it the controlling shares, to Félix Amillot, a businessman of Christian and French heritage with whom the Nazis would not interfere. Thus, Coco failed to secure control over Parfum Chanel from the Wertheimers during the war, but the entire incident evinced her willing to use anti-Semitic laws for personal gain. But Chanel's support for the Nazi regime and its ideology extended further. It is now clear that from 1940, she was deeply involved with the Sicherheitsdienst, or SD, an internal security service force within Nazi Germany and the occupied parts of Europe, which was headed in Western Europe by Walter Schellenberg. The SD is less notorious today than the dreaded Gestapo amongst the German security forces, but its range of operations and overall control of Nazi policing in countries like France was actually considerably greater than those of the Gestapo throughout the Second World War. Chanel had evidently come into contact with the SD and Schellenberg very soon after the Nazis occupied Paris in the mid-summer of 1940. It seems beyond dispute today that she was an SD agent in Paris from that time onwards for the next four years. Given the nature of this activity, much of the documentary records for this clandestine work would necessarily have been destroyed as the Nazis burnt their records in the French capital in advance of the Allied capture of the city in the late summer of 1944, but documents have been found in recent years which refer to Chanel as Agent F7124 in occupied France. Some time after the German occupation of Paris in 1940, Coco began a relationship with Baron Hans Gunther von Dinklage. Here she founded her first official boutique in the French capital shortly after the war ended.

The post-war years were not ones of automatic recovery for the European economy. The years between 1918 and 1923 could be considered in many ways just as difficult as the four years of the First World War, as the Spanish flu swept across Europe in 1918 and 1919, killing millions as it did, while civil wars, revolutions and wars of independence ravaged nearly half of Europe's nations, notably Russia, Germany, Turkey and the many constituent nations of the former Austro-Hungarian Empire. In this environment, luxury brands like Chanel faced a challenging time, yet this was also an age in which the upper class's wealth was relatively impregnable coco consequently benefited from her clientele being able to continue to buy her products in the late 1910s and early 1920s indeed she actively courted the upper class and middle class intellectuals and bohemians in france and beyond realizing that who wore her clothes was nearly as important as the quality of the products themselves. It was an early indication of her awareness of the importance of effective marketing, and Chanel was in essence a prophet of 20th century branding techniques in many ways. As the 1910s gave way to the 1920s, the Chanel brand and company really began to take off. This was aided by the economic boom which began from the early 1920s onwards, as the political situation across Europe began to stabilize. Chanel was a great beneficiary of the roaring 20s, both because people had disposable income to spend on designer clothing and apparel, and also because Chanel's designs were of their time for the more liberal 1920s. Chanel clothing became particularly prized by flappers, the name given to young women in the 1920s, who preferred less conservative clothing and who enjoyed jazz music, socializing, and who had a much less conservative outlook on drinking, sexual activity, and public conduct than the older generations. In tandem, Chanel's profit and business operations began to expand significantly. By 1927, for instance, she had expanded to occupy a block of buildings, running from No. 23 to No. 31 on the Rue Cambon in central Paris, while outlets were opened in other towns and cities as demand increased amongst the middle classes. The 1920s also saw Chanel launch two of its most iconic products. In 1921, Coco released the company's first perfume. Chanel No. 5, as she named it, was a fragrance devised by the Franco-Russian chemist Ernest Beau. It was a balance of the delicate fragrances often worn by women of high society and the more daring, muskier scents favoured by courtesans, thus appealing to a range of women in the more sexually liberated 1920s. The bottle in which Chanel was sold, from its earliest days, has become an iconic part of its brand, with a striking square, glass bottle, although the bevel has changed over the decades. The inspiration for this seems to have come from the toiletries, which Kappel used to carry with him when he and Chanel were romantically involved in the 1900s and 1910s. These were held in plainer bottles than the elaborate cut glass perfume bottles sold to women by many fashion houses. Chanel evidently liked the simplicity of the aftershave bottles and used them for Chanel No. 5, although it has also been argued that she was effectively mimicking the design of a whiskey decanter. Finally, the name was derived from the fact that when Beau had drawn up a number of different scents to present to Chanel, the one which she liked and which became Chanel No. 5 was simply the one located in the fifth file presented to her. From such rudimentary beginnings was Chanel No. 5 born. Chanel used a clever marketing campaign, whereby she first gave bottles of the fragrance to high society friends to generate interest in it. When it finally went to market in her boutiques, sales of the much-hyped perfume were immediately high nearly as iconic as chanel number five was the little black dress which chanel began to sell in 1926 although black dresses had been a luxury item of high society during the renaissance and baroque periods black clothing had become synonymous in the 19th century with funerals and with mourning. Chanel hoped to reverse this development, arguing that black dresses should be the height of fashion. As with all Chanel products by the mid-1920s, marketing was key. When the company began selling its first little black dress, it was accompanied by a spread in Vogue magazine with a picture of the short, calf-length, straight and relatively simple piece of clothing. Alongside this, Vogue ran an editorial in which it hypothesized that Chanel's little black dress was set to become the fashion equivalent of Henry Ford's Model T car, an item of high fashion which could be produced in bulk and so sold at a reasonable price, a development which the magazine supposed would make it, quote,

a sort of uniform for all women of taste. They were not entirely wrong. Sales of Chanel's Little Black Dress were impressive, beginning to overturn the Victorian conception of black dresses being funerary and mourning attire. On the back of the success of Chanel No. 5 and the Little Black Dress, Chanel's product range continued to expand in the 1920s and into the early 1930s, unhindered to a large extent by the Great Depression, which followed the Wall Street crash of the autumn of 1929. Her designs were eclectic. For instance, in the early 1920s, Coco began experimenting with Slavic-inspired designs. One evening dress, which Chanel began selling in 1922, came with a matching babushka headscarf. This was in part owing to her partnership with the embroidery house Kitmir, which had been established in Paris in the post-war period by Grand Duchess Maria Pavlovna, a granddaughter of Tsar Alexander II, who had ruled Russia as a reformer from 1855 until his assassination in 1881. Maria had fled her homeland with the onset of the Russian Revolution in 1917 and now brought an Eastern fashion aesthetic to the French capital, which Coco incorporated into her clothing range in the 1920s. Eventually the collaboration between Chanel and Kitmir ended in 1928, when the Grand Duchess sold the business, which had been poorly managed and haemorrhaged money despite the success of its association with Chanel. Other successes of Chanel in the 1920s included the introduction of the first women's suit range, while the first Chanel hand
bag, the design of which was inspired, like so many other Chanel products, by the utility and
simplicity of male soldiers' bags, was launched in 1929. One final element of Chanel's life which
is worth noting for its lasting impact on society was sunbathing. For centuries in early modern
Europe and into the 19th century and early 20th century, pale skin had been viewed as the

I'll see you next time. impact on society was sunbathing. For centuries in early modern Europe and into the 19th century and early 20th century, pale skin had been viewed as the ideal of female beauty. Cosmetics were designed to lighten the skin, and upper-class women and aristocrats generally tended to avoid the sun and its tanning effect. Yet there was a growing amount of scientific information to suggest that a certain amount of direct sunlight was good for avoiding diseases like rickets. Thus there was a movement towards what we would now call sunbathing in the 1900s and 1910s, but it was Chanel who really popularized the notion in the 1920s. In 1923, while on a cruise on the French Riviera, Coco was sunburned. In the days that followed, as the redness turned to tan,
she was photographed stepping from a boat at the seaside resort of Cannes.
The photos of a woman who by the mid-1920s had developed a reputation as a paragon of fashion and taste
quickly set off a trend whereby having a tan became a positive in European society.

Incredibly, this simple act has had a lasting influence and sunbathing became a 20th century phenomenon in the course of the 1920s chanel began to associate her brand with the most visible aspects of contemporary popular culture this meant anything on stage or on screen hence in 1924 she designed the costumes for the dance opera Le Trin Bleu, directed by the Russian ballet composer Serge Diaghilev and choreographed by Václav Nijinsky. It was the first of numerous stage productions for which Chanel provided costumes and outfits. This pattern continued into the 1930s, and in 1931 Coco headed for Hollywood at the invitation of Samuel Goldwyn, the head of one of the largest studios emerging in Los Angeles at the dawn of the age of the talkies. Goldwyn, a Polish-American businessman, had met Coco while holidaying in Monte Carlo. The deal she reached with Goldwyn was enormous, with the studio head offering her one million dollars, the equivalent of upwards of $100 million in today's money, to visit America twice a year to design clothes for his films. However, while she designed dresses for a number of films in the 1930s and acquired both Marlene Dietrich and Greta Garbo as clients, Chanel found American culture garish and the world of Hollywood films unsophisticated by comparison with interwar Paris. Her arrangement with Goldwyn dwindled as the 1930s progressed. Coco's personal life was tumultuous by the time she was visiting Hollywood in the 1930s. In the post-war years, she was involved in a number of high-profile affairs and relationships. For instance, in the mid-1920s, she was involved with Pierre Reverdy, a French surrealist poet whose work inspired much of the Dada movement. More controversial was her relationship with Paul Iribe between 1931 and his death in 1935. Iribe was a designer and illustrator who had worked for Cecil B. DeMille in Hollywood in the 1920s, notably drawing up the designs for the look of Egypt for DeMille's version of the Ten Commandments in 1923. Between 1933 and 1935, Coco helped finance Irib's journal Le Temois, The Witness, a publication which presented the artworks of Irib and others. The 69 issues of the journal, which appeared in the mid-1930s, were highly political, with Irib demonstrating a charged sense of antipathy towards foreigners in France, a pronounced anti-Semitism and an ultra-nationalist platform. As we will see shortly, this appears not to have conflicted with Chanel's own politics by the 1930s. Beyond her sexual relationships, Chanel continued to mix in bohemian and artistic circles in Paris throughout the 1920s and 1930s. These were hedonistic times, and there was one rumor around Paris at the time that Coco had actually acquired her first name in reference to the prolific quantities of cocaine taken at her parties in the 1920s. While this explanation is probably somewhat spurious, we do know that Chanel developed a habit of injecting herself with morphine on a daily basis in the early 1930s. She would remain addicted to opiates for the remainder of her life. The 1930s saw Chanel branching out further again into women's jewelry. In 1932, the company held an exhibition of its first items, one which made clear that this range would certainly be on the higher end of Chanel's products. The show featured products called the Comet and the Fountain, two necklaces containing multiple diamonds. While this show presented material which was not exactly for general purchase, the 1930s were notable for the manner in which Chanel began to incorporate rhinestones and other smaller and more modest gemstones into the fashion house's dresses and products, something which disseminated into other fashion houses in the years that followed. The end result of all of this rampant growth in the 1920s and early 1930s, and the manner in which Chanel had established itself as the preeminent fashion house in not just France, but in Europe, was a company which had a huge revenue, and that employed upwards of 4,000 people in various capacities, as seamstresses, shop employees, and designers. Only one company could rival it by the mid-1930s. This was the house of Schiaparelli, which had been founded in Paris in 1927 by the Italian aristocrat fashion designer Elsa Schiaparelli. Beginning with knitwear and then moving into avant-garde and surrealist design, Schiaparelli soon followed Chanel's lead in associating with some of Paris and France's leading artistic figures in order to promote her products, notably in Schiaparelli's case the artist and filmmaker Salvador Dali and the poet, playwright and visual artist Jean Cocteau. By the early 1930s, Schiaparelli's range had expanded dramatically. Although many individuals lambasted Schiaparelli's designs in the 1930s as being impractical or even bizarre, she was the only designer in France in the 1930s whose influence and reputation matched that of Chanel. Perhaps if history had not unfolded as it did in Europe in the late 1930s to mid-1940s, Coco and the House of Chanel's enormous success might have continued unmarred by controversy. But a chain of events beginning in Germany and the choices she would make in that decade would ultimately cast a shroud over Coco Chanel's legacy. Early in 1933, after a rapid ascent in political popularity following economic turmoil partially caused by the Wall Street crash and the Great Depression, the Nazi party, led by Adolf Hitler, came to power in Germany. They were determined to reverse what they perceived as the humiliation foisted upon Germany at the end of the First World War and to make their nation the foremost power of continental Europe. This would mean a new war, with France being one of the primary targets, not just to recover the provinces of Alsace and Lorraine, which had been lost following the First World War, but also to dominate Germany's western neighbor. Initially, the Nazis had anticipated needing a gradual military build-up to accomplish their aims, intending to wait until 1941 or 1942 to initiate full-scale war. But when the European powers were both sluggish to re-arm and put up little resistance to Germany's annexation of Austria and Czechoslovakia in 1938 and 1939 respectively, Hitler moved the schedule forward and in September 1939 invaded Poland. This was a step too far for both Britain and France, and they declared war on Germany in response. This was the beginning of what would become the Second World War. For Coco Chanel and many other French people, the outbreak of the war in the autumn of 1939 impacted little on their lives to begin with. The fighting was largely confined to Poland, with only limited border skirmishes and forays taking place along the Franco-German border. Hence, while the news of the German occupation of Poland was concerning for many in Paris, beyond the obvious signs of military mobilization and the imposition of rationing, life went on much as it previously had in the final months of 1939 and into early 1940. However, normal life came to a shuddering halt in the late spring, as the Nazis first conducted a blistering campaign against Denmark and Norway, and then proceeded to invade the Low Countries and northeastern France. The speed of the German advance caught both the French military and the British expeditionary force in northern France by surprise, and within weeks the Nazis were closing in on the capital. The French government fled on the 10th of June, and the Germans occupied the city four days later. A collaborationist government negotiated surrender terms, whereby the southern half of France would be ruled by a semi-autonomous government based out of the town of Vichy, where Chanel had spent summers performing in the 1900s. But Paris was to be occupied by the Nazis for the next four years. It was a period during which countless French men and women like Coco Chanel would have to decide whether to resist the regime or collaborate with it. As we will see, Coco Chanel chose the latter. Upon the commencement of war in September 1939, Chanel had released a public statement affirming her belief that a period of renewed warfare across Europe was not a time for high fashion, and that she consequently intended to close all of her shops, except for the flagship enterprise on the Rue de Cambon. This was not necessarily an unreasonable step to take. After all, with rationing being introduced across the continent, and trade networks disintegrating as nations went to war with each other, there would be a lack of raw materials available to make high-end fashion, and few people would have been comfortable investing in conspicuous signs of wealth in such straightened times. But it has also been suggested that Chanel used the outbreak of the war to exact revenge on her employees, many of whom had engaged in disputes over working conditions and pay in 1936, during a general strike amongst the French labor unions. Whatever her motives might have been, we know what fate ultimately befell Chanel's employees. Nearly 4,000 people found themselves out of work from the late autumn of 1939 onwards. The house of Chanel had effectively been furloughed until the end of the war. This has raised questions as to what Chanel was doing, herself, throughout the conflict, especially after the occupation of Paris in the summer of 1940. The answer, revealed by a number of sources in recent times, is not as one would hope for a historical figure of such cultural influence. Chanel would spend much of the war consorting and collaborating with the Nazi regime. Her willingness to countenance the Nazis in France was seen early on when she took up residence in the Hotel Ritz on the Place Vendôme in central Paris. Following the occupation of the French capital in June 1940, the Ritz had quickly become a favorite haunt of the leading members of the occupation Nazi government. Thus, anyone staying here or consorting with other guests was making a clear statement in favor of the German takeover of France and Nazi ideology. Moreover, this interpretation of Chanel's choice in residence is aligned with Chanel's own attested political and racial views by the early 1940s, which were foreshadowed by her relationship with Irib and her support for his ultra-nationalist journal, The Witness, in the 1930s. As such, her decision to shut Chanel's stores and operations across France in the autumn of 1939 has been viewed by some historians as part of an effort to undermine her Jewish business partners. One particular instance of such efforts stands out, her attempt in 1941 to use anti-Semitic laws in Nazi-occupied France to steal company profits from her Jewish business partner, whose descendants, in a twist of fate, would go on to own the Chanel brand to this day. While Chanel was the face and primary owner of the House of Chanel, she had had a significant business partner since the early 1920s. This was Pierre Wertheimer, a scion of a German-Jewish family who had moved from the Alsace region around the Franco-German border to Paris following the outbreak of the Franco-Prussian War in 1870. They remained in the French capital after the swift Prussian victory in that war and Pierre was born in the city in 1888. As a young man in his 20s, Pierre became involved in the cosmetics industry in France, and helped develop a dry rouge for the notable French makeup company Bourgeois. Such was the success of this product that Wertheimer rose quickly within Bourgeois, and in 1917 he and his brother Paul had become directors thereof. It was just a few short years later that Chanel and Wertheimer began negotiations to use Wertheimer's business contacts and industry links to help market Chanel No. 5 globally. Eventually, Chanel, Wertheimer and several other French and Jewish investors in the cosmetics and fragrance industry reached an arrangement in the mid-1920s whereby the Wertheimers would effectively manufacture, distribute and otherwise manage Chanel No. 5. They would receive 70% of the profits in return for effectively running Parfum Chanel. As the subsidiary became known, Coco would become an all-but-silent partner. She would have to do very little henceforth and would receive 10% of all profits based on her intellectual copyright of the fragrance and branding. While many would have been happy with this arrangement, as the Wertheimers expanded the reach of Chanel No. 5 and increased sales in the course of the 1920s and 1930s, Coco became increasingly disgruntled. In 1935, she had already attempted to sue Pierre and his family, but the suit had failed as Coco was trying to dispute the validity of a binding legal agreement, which she had freely entered into years ago. She now set out to use the occupation of France by the Nazis and the pronounced anti-Semitism of the new regime against the Wertheimers and their Jewish heritage. Beginning in 1941, she began petitioning the German occupation government in France to be given sole control of Parfum Chanel, using a Nazi law which allowed people of allegedly Aryan race to take over Jewish businesses. She was also able to claim that the Wertheimers had abandoned the business when they fled to the United States to avoid Nazi persecution in 1940. However, she was surprised to learn in the course of the summer and autumn of 1941 that Wertheimer had taken steps to prevent her seizing the company. Before he and his family fled France, he had transferred his 70% stake, and with it the controlling shares, to Félix Amillot, a businessman of Christian and French heritage with whom the Nazis would not interfere. Thus, Coco failed to secure control over Parfum Chanel from the Wertheimers during the war, but the entire incident evinced her willing to use anti-Semitic laws for personal gain. But Chanel's support for the Nazi regime and its ideology extended further. It is now clear that from 1940, she was deeply involved with the Sicherheitsdienst, or SD, an internal security service force within Nazi Germany and the occupied parts of Europe, which was headed in Western Europe by Walter Schellenberg. The SD is less notorious today than the dreaded Gestapo amongst the German security forces, but its range of operations and overall control of Nazi policing in countries like France was actually considerably greater than those of the Gestapo throughout the Second World War. Chanel had evidently come into contact with the SD and Schellenberg very soon after the Nazis occupied Paris in the mid-summer of 1940. It seems beyond dispute today that she was an SD agent in Paris from that time onwards for the next four years. Given the nature of this activity, much of the documentary records for this clandestine work would necessarily have been destroyed as the Nazis burnt their records in the French capital in advance of the Allied capture of the city in the late summer of 1944, but documents
have been found in recent years which refer to Chanel as Agent F7124 in occupied France.
This, along with ancillary information, points clearly to Chanel's collaboration with the
Nazi regime.
Another aspect of this entanglement with the Third Reich concerned Chanel's love life.

Some time after the German occupation of Paris in 1940, Coco began a relationship with Baron Hans Gunther von Dinklage. Von Dinklage was a German diplomatic attaché to the occupation regime in the French capital and also served as an Abwehr spy in Paris. He had close contacts with Heinrich Himmler, the head of the German SS and one of the senior-most members of the entire Nazi regime, and Joseph Goebbels, the German propaganda minister who had a major role in spying, propaganda and legal matters in France from 1940 onwards. By 1942, Chanel and von Dinklage were romantically involved with each other, and this continued throughout the German occupation of France and beyond. There is no doubting that this relationship spilled over from their personal lives into cooperation on various espionage and spying activities in Western Europe during the war. Perhaps the most explicit sign of this is that Chanel was considered for a time as a potential negotiator and liaison between the British Prime Minister, Winston Churchill, and elements within the Nazi regime led by Himmler, who considered that it might be best to negotiate surrender terms with the Western Allies rather than face the possibility of the Soviet Union overrunning Germany. This did not lead to constructive talks, but as we will see shortly, Churchill did intervene on Chanel's behalf in the closing stages of the war and following its conclusion in the summer of 1945. In some instances, there is even more explicit evidence of Chanel's involvement in various clandestine Nazi activities. One such case was Operation Modelhut, a planned spy operation which was to be part of a speculative initiative which the Nazi regime began considering in 1942, to take control of Spain, a country which was headed by the fascist regime of General Francisco Franco, but which had decided to remain neutral in the war. Operation Modelhut, or Model Hat, involved Chanel as an Abwehr or Nazi counterintelligence agent heading to Madrid in 1943 to attempt to convince the British ambassador in the Spanish capital, Sir Samuel Hoare, that the Nazis were considering a negotiated peace. The mission subsequently led to Chanel heading to Berlin to meet with Schellenberg along with von Dinklage concerning the scheme, one which became extremely complicated as there were suggestions that Chanel could employ her contacts in Britain, which reached all the way up to Churchill, to aid in any effort by the Nazi regime to win the war diplomatically, even as its armed forces were suffering enormous reverses on all fronts against the Allied nations. However, Operation Model Hut was a dead letter from its

very inception. As a young Anglo-Italian woman with whom Chanel had originally traveled to Madrid, Vera Bat Lombardi ultimately betrayed details of the plan when they reached Spain. In the end, Chanel's efforts to aid the Nazi regime in its decline served only in retrospect to reveal her as an agent of Nazism and Hitler's regime in occupied France. By early 1944, Chanel must have been aware that her collaboration with the Nazi regime and the German security forces during the occupation of France could become extremely problematic for her before long. While the first years of the Second World War were marked by a run of immense victories for German armies across Europe, things had run aground for the Nazis in the winter of 1941, as the German advance into Russia stalled outside Moscow and Leningrad, and the United States then entered the war on the side of Britain and the Soviet Union, following the attack on its Pacific fleet in Pearl Harbor by Germany's ally, the Empire of Japan, early that December. 1942 and early 1943 were the tipping point of the war, as the Western Allies achieved victory over Germany and Italy in the North African theater, and the Germans lost the Battle of Stalingrad to the Soviet Union in a conflict which became the crucible of the war. With a southern front established in Sicily in the summer of 1943, the Allies' thoughts turned to invading France and ending the Nazi occupation of the north of the country. The D-Day landings opened the Western Front in early June of 1944, with Paris one of the Allies' primary targets. Though fierce fighting across northern France slowed matters, the Americans, British forces, Canadians and their allies arrived outside Paris on 19 August 1944. Just six days later, the Nazi occupation of the French capital was finally ended after four long years. As elsewhere across Europe in 1944 and 1945, public attention quickly turned to identifying those who had collaborated with the Nazis and how they should be punished for doing so.

It was not long before Chanel's activities during the Nazi occupation of Paris were called into question. In the autumn of 1943, the Free French Government, based out of France's colony of Algeria, had established the Commission d'Apparition, a judicial committee which was to oversee the prosecution of French collaborationist war criminals, once France was liberated from Nazi rule. Although the number of people prosecuted by the Commission was dwarfed by the number of summary executions carried out in France in 1944 and 1945, it did prosecute tens of thousands of individuals. Amongst those questioned by the Commission was Coco Chanel, who was first
detained and questioned by the Commission in September 1944, a few weeks after the liberation
of Paris. However, Chanel's was not a straightforward case. She had not held any public position within
the Nazi occupation government. There was furthermore no documentation to hand at the time

Thank you for watching. Chanel's was not a straightforward case. She had not held any public position within the Nazi occupation government. There was furthermore no documentation to hand at the time which proved beyond a doubt Chanel's involvement with Schellenberg's SD or the Abwehr. While her relationship with von Dinklage and her known fraternization with the upper brass of the Nazi occupation government at the Hotel Ritz throughout the war, certainly compromised public perceptions of Chanel, there was little evidence with which to charge her with a serious crime. Moreover, it has been widely speculated ever since that Winston Churchill, who had known Chanel for many years prior to the war, intervened on her behalf to have Coco released from the scrutiny of the Commission d'Apparition. This remains a speculative claim however and there is no concrete evidence to confirm this. With the end of the war a tricky business issue also quickly arose for Chanel. Pierre Wertheimer and his family had fled to the United States in the wake of the advancing Nazi regime in 1940 but while Chanel had sought to use the Wertheimers' Jewish

heritage against them under Nazi law to gain control of Parfum Chanel, during the German occupation of France, Pierre's decision to transfer ownership of the company to Félix Amiour before his family left Europe had foiled her plans. Now, in the aftermath of the conflict, the dispute arose yet again. Curiously, Chanel decided to hire René de Chambrun, the son-in-law of Pierre Laval, the former Prime Minister of Vichy France, who had been executed in October 1945 on account of his role in the collaborationist regime. The case was a troubling one for both parties. Chanel did not wish to have her wartime record dragged out in public while for wertheimer although he had done nothing wrong the reality was that if coco's collaboration with the nazis was aired extensively in public it would quite possibly lead to a boycott of chanel perfumes and a drastic decline in his own profit margins accordingly a settlement was concluded in the early summer of, without airing the company's unsavory secrets in public, whereby Wertheimer would retain the controlling interest at Parfum Chanel, but Coco herself would continue to receive 2% of the profits from any and all future sales, as well as other financial investments. It might seem as though this was a financial defeat, but in reality, the sums involved made Coco Chanel one of the wealthiest women of the 1950s and 1960s. While Chanel had managed to rescue a piecemeal victory from the jaws of defeat when it came to Parfum Chanel in the years immediately following the end of the Second World War, her own public reputation was severely damaged by her wartime conduct within france she decided to leave as a result already in 1945 once the charges against her were dropped and she was released from a brief imprisonment she headed with von dinklage for switzerland a nation which had remained neutral throughout the war and which had the dubious distinction of becoming a haven for many naz war criminals during the late 1940s and early 1950s. She lived in the town of Lausanne, on the northern shores of Lac-Lemain, just over 25 kilometers from the eastern border of France, where she was constantly perturbed by the rise of new fashion houses in France, Spain and Italy, such as those of Christian Dior and Cristobal Balenciaga, whose designs Chanel deemed to be far too rigid and conservative in comparison with the House of Chanel's clothing and products in the interwar period of the 1920s and 1930s. Nevertheless, the House of Chanel's reputation was badly damaged by the war, and Coco had little opportunity to revive it through to the mid-1950s. Indeed, as late as 1949, she was called to give testimony in a French court, where she was attacked for her links to the Nazis in the trial of Baron Louis de Vaufréland, a former Nazi intelligence agent. Interestingly, on this occasion, Chanel defended her character by claiming that Duff Cooper, a former British ambassador to France following the liberation of the country in 1944, and a diplomat appointed by Winston Churchill, could provide a character reference for her wartime conduct should it be needed. Exactly what she meant and what Cooper might have revealed remains frustratingly unclear to the present day. There was something of a coda in the early 1950s to Chanel's involvement with the Nazis and the security services in occupied France during the Second World War. After the war had ended, Walter Schellenberg, the chief of foreign intelligence gathering within the SD, and an individual that Chanel had some contact with during the war, though the extent of which cannot be fully determined, was arrested in Germany. Schellenberg was a senior security agent, but he was not implicated in the high-level decision-making that had either fomented the war or led to the Holocaust of Europe's Jews and other genocidal policies. Although he clearly had engaged in genocidal activity, not least his involvement in managing the activities of the Einsatzgruppen death squads on the Eastern Front following the invasion of the Soviet Union in 1941. Nevertheless, he did not stand trial in the first trial of the leaders of the Nazi regime at Nuremberg from late 1945 onwards. Instead, he stood trial during the Ministry's trial of second-tier Nazi ministers in 1948. It appears that Schoenel was funneling financial aid to his wife and children during this period of imprisonment. Owing to his having provided extensive evidence on the actions of the SS and the Gestapo, Schellenberg escaped with a sentence of six years in prison when judgment was made in 1949. In reality, however, he only served two years before being released on health grounds, owing to an ultimately fatal liver condition. After his release, he headed for Switzerland, where he may have been entertained by Coco for a time, before ultimately settling in Turin in Italy, where he died in March 1952. When he died, it was Coco Chanel who paid for his funeral and offered further financial support to his widow. There can be few clearer statements of Chanel's sympathies and involvement with the Nazi security services during the Second World War than her continuing support for Schellenberg throughout the late 1940s and early 1950s. Chanel was not content to live in quiet retirement in Switzerland for the remainder of her life, even though by the mid-1950s she was entering her early 70s. Moreover, with the intensification of the Cold War in the 1950s and the passage of time since the Nazi era, many people who had collaborated with Hitler and the Third Reich were being reintegrated into society across Europe. Thus, in 1954, Chanel sought to re-establish her fashion house back in France. And in order to do so, she turned to a very peculiar source for financing, her long-time rival and estranged business partner, Pierre Wertheimer. It was Wertheimer who provided the necessary financing for Chanel to launch a new fashion range in 1954, one which was critically lauded across the North American and European fashion worlds, despite continuing reservations in many circles about welcoming Chanel back openly into the world of couture in light of her wartime conduct. In particular, the support of magazines such as Vogue and Life contributed to the rebirth of the House of Chanel and ensured that the company would re-emerge as one of the leading fashion companies of the 1950s and 1960s. The next years were ones of renewed success for Coco Chanel. In 1957, the company won the Fashion Oscar at the Fashion Awards in the city of Dallas in Texas. These were innovative years. For instance, the 1950s saw Chanel move into producing a wide range of jewelry items, while the house's first male fragrance, or Eau de Toilette, was released in the mid-1950s. 1955 also witnessed the initial release of Chanel's range of leather handbags.
